data.title: Nikkei Falls 3.0%, Dragged by Electronics, Pharma, Auto StocksMarket Talk
data.description: Japanese stocks ended lower as growing U.S.-China trade tensions weighed on the global economic outlook. Electronics, pharmaceutical and auto shares led the losses. Sony Group dropped 7.4%, Chugai Pharmaceutical lost 6.4%, and Nissan Motor shed 6.3%. The Nikkei Stock Average fell 3.0% to 33585.58. Tariff-related headlines remain in focus. USD/JPY is at 144.13, down from 144.44 as of Thursday 5 p.m. Eastern time. The 2-year Japanese government bond yield dropped 7 bps to 0.605%, the 10-year yield fell 2.5 bps to 1.345% while the 20-year yield rose 6 bps at 2.365%. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

data.title: U.K. Economy Shows Signs of Strength But Tariffs Darken Outlook2nd Update
data.description:
By Joshua Kirby

The U.K. economy is growing more strongly than expected but U.S. President Trump's barrage of tariff hikes and pauses threaten to stymie further acceleration ahead.

Gross domestic product increased by 0.5% in February, figures from the Office for National Statistics showed Friday. That was a better performance than the marginal growth economists had expected, and means the U.K. economy is very likely to record growth over the first quarter as a whole, barring an unexpectedly large reversal in March.

February's growth was driven by strong output from Britain's factories, with manufacturing production unexpectedly rising 2.2% on month on the back of a rise in output from auto plants, as well as in electronics and pharmaceuticals, the ONS said.

Still, strength in manufacturing may be in part down to firms seeing a temporary rush of orders aiming to beat Washington's planned tariffs.

"It's possible the industrial sector is benefiting from tariff front-running," Capital Economics' Ruth Gregory said in a note.

"February's resilience is unlikely to last," she said, pointing to the wider global economic malaise stemming from uncertainty around Washington's trade policy.

After Trump last week unveiled a sweeping package of tariffs on goods imported into the U.S., London's FTSE 100 index suffered four straight days of losses, enduring on Monday its worst trading since the Covid-19 pandemic. Stocks rallied later in the week after Trump agreed to halt a central part of the tariff package amid the turmoil roiling markets at home and across the globe.

But a 10% tariff will remain in place on U.K. goods, as will a separate 25% duty on cars and metals. At more than 15% of the total, the U.S. is the largest export market for the U.K. goods, of which more than 58 billion pounds ($75.23 billion) worth crossed the Atlantic to America in the 12 months to last September, according to figures from the government's business department.

That makes the U.K. less exposed to tariffs than some neighbors, such as Germany, which sent 160 billion euros of goods to the U.S. last year, or Ireland, home to the operations of many American drugmakers. British goods exports also pale next to services exports to the U.S., worth some 124 billion pounds a year, and not liable to Trump's levies. But even a 10% tariff could knock Britain's already fragile economic growth.

And policymakers at the Bank of England could be forced into cutting interest rates more quickly than they had foreseen in a bid to lift some of the smog clouding activity in the country.

"The damaging aftereffects from this [tariff] turbulence will be felt for some time, with the resulting global uncertainty likely to further undermine the U.K. economy," said Suren Thiru, economics director at the Institute of Chartered Accountants in England and Wales. Businesses will also suffer from an increase in payroll costs from April, Thiru noted.

"The greater global financial and economic instability caused by the US tariff announcements makes a May rate cut look more likely than not, by further fuelling rate setters' concerns over the underlying resilience of the U.K. economy," Thiru said.

A more general trade war is likely to mean slower growth ahead for the U.K. economy Sustained high tariffs on British goods entering the U.S. market, as well as indirect effects from a chillier global trade landscape, could mean GDP expanding just 0.7% this year, compared with 1.5% in case tariffs were not implemented, according to calculations by the National Institute of Economic and Social Research, a think tank. Growth would be even feebler next year in that scenario, the NIESR said in a report.

"History shows how devastating a full-scale tariff war can be," NIESR economist Ahmet Kaya said.

The government last week said it would invite British businesses to shape the response to tariffs, and began compiling a list of American goods that could be targeted in retaliation.

"We are pressing the case for a trading relationship that supports businesses on both sides of the Atlantic," trade minister Jonathan Reynolds said.

Prime Minister Keir Starmer, meanwhile, this week said New York-based entertainment giant Comcast NBCUniversal would build a new theme park outside London, an investment he said would boost the British economy by billions of pounds.

"It will boost our world-class creative industries," he said. "It will drive growth across the country."

Write to Joshua Kirby at joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby

data.title: South Korean Auto Stocks Fall Sharply Amid Tariff Woes
data.description:
By Kwanwoo Jun

South Korean auto stocks led losses on the country's main stock exchange Friday, as U.S. tariffs on car imports continued to weigh on sentiment.

Shares of carmakers Hyundai Motor and Kia fell 5.1% and 7.0%, respectively, underperforming the benchmark Kospi's 0.5% decline. The Friday declines erased gains of around 5% in the prior session following President Trump's announcement of a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs on most trading partners.

Trump's tariff pause doesn't apply to the 25% U.S. auto levy already effective since early April.

The U.S. auto tariff could cost Hyundai Motor an extra expense of 8.800 trillion won, equivalent to $6.05 billion, annually, Shinhan Securities analyst K.R. Park said in a research note Friday. Park trimmed his target price for the stock by 6.9% to 270,000 won.

Kia, a sibling company of Hyundai Motor, on Thursday lowered its 2030 annual vehicle sales target to 4.19 million units from 4.30 million units previously.

South Korea's government earlier this week announced emergency-support measures for the country's auto sector to help cushion the impact of the tariffs, which it said will cause "significant" damage to local automakers and parts manufacturers.

The multibillion-dollar aid package by the trade ministry includes financial support, tax breaks and subsidies for carmakers.

South Korea's car exports to the U.S. stood at $34.7 billion in 2024, accounting for nearly half of its total auto exports.

Write to Kwanwoo Jun at kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com

data.title: Macmahon Holdings' Shares Trading 'Cheaply' Compared to Peers, Says Euroz Hartleys
data.description:
Macmahon Holdings is trading at 5.1 times its price-to-earnings ratio at an approximately 8.4% earnings per share compound annual growth rate, screening "cheaply" compared to its peers, said Euroz Hartleys in a Thursday note.

The company has secured a three-year extension for its contract at the Byerwen coking coal mine in Queensland, contributing about AU$900 million to Macmahon's secured order book.

The research firm noted that the extension comes with no additional capital expenditure required as the company already has existing staff and mining fleet

at the operation.

Euroz Hartleys maintained its buy recommendation and AU$0.43 price target for Macmahon Holdings, both under review.

data.title: Stellantis Shipments Fall 9% on Lower North American ProductionUpdate
data.description:
By Dominic Chopping

Stellantis vehicle shipments fell 9% in the first quarter of 2025, when the Jeep and Ram-maker extended holiday shutdowns at North American plants in January.

The auto company, which also houses brands including Chrysler, Fiat and Peugeot, said global shipments--vehicle deliveries to dealers, distributors, or directly from the company to retail and fleet customers--fell to an estimated 1.2 million units.

In North America, vehicle shipments fell 20% in the quarter. Around 82,000 fewer cars were shipped than in the same quarter a year earlier on lower production due to the shutdowns, as well as the initial ramp up of its updated 2025 Ram heavy duty trucks.

Stellantis recently completed a process to reduce bloated inventories in the U.S. by increasing incentives for customers and cutting the number of cars it shipped to its dealers. Normalized dealer inventory levels were maintained during the quarter, it said.

The Jeep Compass, Grand Cherokee and Ram 1500/2500 each posted on-year increases sales volumes of more than 10% in the quarter. The company noted that March's new retail orders in the U.S. were at the highest level since July 2023.

Also hitting shipments in the quarter was the impact of product transitions and lower light commercial vehicle volumes in Europe.

Europe shipments fell by 47,000 vehicles, representing an 8% decline on year.

Across South America, Middle East & Africa, China and India & Asia Pacific, shipments grew by 4%, mainly driven by higher volumes in South America which more than offset declines elsewhere.

Despite the overall decline in global shipments, Stellantis launched new models in the quarter that included the Citroen C3 Aircross, Opel Frontera, Fiat Grande Panda, and the Ram 2500 and 3500 heavy-duty trucks, which it said helped drive positive momentum in order intake.

U.S. President Trump's 25% tariffs on automobile imports kicked in at the beginning of April and automakers are scrambling to assess the impact on their business. Last week, Stellantis said it was pausing production at its auto assembly factories in Mexico and Canada after the tariffs came into effect.

It has also extended its employee-discount program to the broader U.S. public on most new models as it moves to stoke sales, following a similar move by Ford.

Write to Dominic Chopping at dominic.chopping@wsj.com

data.title: German Bunds Continue to Outperform U.S. TreasurysMarket Talk
data.description: German Bunds and other eurozone government bond yields are little changed in early trading, outperforming U.S. Treasurys whose yields rise. "Earlier this week we noticed that the latest bout of risk-off has re-affirmed the international safe-haven status of German sovereign paper relative to U.S. Treasurys," Citi Research's Andrea Appeddu says in a note. The 10-year Bund yield is trading 0.4 basis point higher at 2.595%, while the 10-year Treasury yield is up 6.5 basis points, last trading at 4.458%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Kinetic Engineering Invests INR50 Million in Subsidiary
data.description:
Kinetic Engineering has invested 50 million Indian rupees in its subsidiary Kinetic Watts and Volts, according to a Friday filing to the BSE.

The company has subscribed to 5 million equity shares of 10 rupees each. With this, the company's shareholding in Kinetic Watts has increased to 66.1% from 61.63%.

Shares of the company were up nearly 2% in recent trade.

data.title: Yue Yuen Industrial Logs 0.7% Drop in March Operating Revenue
data.description:
Yue Yuen Industrial recorded an operating revenue of $657.7 million in March, down 0.7% from a year prior, a Thursday filing with the Hong Kong bourse said.

In the first quarter of 2025, operating revenue rose by 1.3% year over year to $2.03 billion.

data.title: Toyota Financial Services to issue over 3-year bonds, bankers say
data.description:
India's Toyota Financial Services plans to raise 5 billion rupees ($58.04 million) through the sale of bonds maturing in three years and two months, three bankers said on Friday.

The company has invited bids from bankers and investors for the issue on Tuesday, they said.

Toyota Financial Services did not immediately reply to a Reuters email seeking comment.

Here is the list of deals reported so far on April 11:

data.title: Tokyo Shares Drop As US Tariffs On China Climb To 145%
data.description:
Japanese shares fell Friday after Trump flagged "transition problems" from tariffs, following a White House update that US levies on China rose to 145%.

The Nikkei 225 fell 2.96%, or 1,023.42 points, to end at 33,585.58.

Trump said tariffs would bring "transition problems" but called them "a beautiful thing" and insisted the US is "in very good shape."

The White House memo showed US tariffs on China have climbed to 145%, including a new 125% charge aimed at addressing the trade deficit and punishing Beijing's retaliation.

A separate 20% levy targets China's role in fentanyl trafficking. Other imports, including solar panel materials, are already taxed.

Among corporate movers, Haseko launched a tender offer for Wood Friends to make it a wholly owned unit, potentially leading to delisting.

It will pursue a cash-out or share consolidation depending on voting rights. Wood Friends' board backs the move.

Japan Airlines raised 178.9 billion yen via two subordinated bond tranches to fund aircraft purchases. The bonds offer 3.218% and 4.124% interest, callable in 2030 and 2035, respectively.

Shift found fraudulent subsidy claims at subsidiary Infinic, linked to its former director before the 2023 acquisition. The firm has reported the case and is tightening controls.

data.title: Trifork Wins Oman Healthcare System Project
data.description:
Trifork was awarded a contract by the Ministry of Health in Oman to modernize the country's healthcare system.

The software company will develop a revenue cycle management system and upgrade Oman's National Health Information Exchange, according to a Friday release.

The project will be implemented in phases over two years, starting with a six-month proof of concept.

data.title: Spain's Sanchez calls for more balanced relationship between EU, China during visit
data.description:
Spain favours a more balanced relationship between the European Union and China based on negotiations to resolve differences and cooperation in areas of common interest, Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez said on Friday in Beijing after meeting with Chinese President Xi Jinping.

Sanchez's visit aims to forge closer economic and political ties with Beijing amid the global fallout of U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff policy, seeking to position Spain as an interlocutor between China and the European Union and attract Chinese investment.

Spanish officials have rejected a U.S. warning that moving closer to the Asian country would be "cutting your own throat".

At the start of their meeting, Sanchez said that Spain saw China as a partner to the EU. Officially, the bloc described China as a "partner for cooperation, an economic competitor and a systemic rival" in its 2019 strategy.

"We want to work on issues of common interest, promoting trade and investment in a balanced way to benefit the development of our countries from our respective visions, and we want to foster closer ties between our societies," Sanchez told Xi.

The Chinese leader underlined that this was Sanchez's third visit in as many years to the country, adding that strengthening bilateral relations in the current global context promoted peace, stability and prosperity.

data.title: Yuzhou Group Logs 872 Million Yuan in March Sales
data.description:
Yuzhou Group recorded sales of 872 million yuan in March, with a corresponding sold gross floor area of 54,501 square meters, a Thursday filing with the Hong Kong bourse said.

The property firm's average selling price for the month was 16,000 yuan per square meter.

In the first quarter of 2025, sales and sold GFA reached 1.93 billion yuan and 125,862 square meters, respectively.

As of March 31, the company saw around 2 million yuan of subscribed sales.

data.title: ML System Appoints Dawid Cycon As Deputy CEO
data.description:
ML System SA :

  • APPOINTS DAWID CYCON AS DEPUTY CEO

data.title: Akme Fintrade Inks Deal With Finayo to Offer Financing of Electric Vehicles
data.description:
Akme Fintrade (India) has partnered with India-based startup Kilpi Technology (FINAYO) and Services for the financing of electric vehicles, according to a Friday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

The deal expands the company's product offerings to include financing for electric vehicles.

Shares of the company were up over 2% in recent trade.

data.title: Flows into Indian equity mutual funds slump to 11-month low in March
data.description:
Flows into India's equity mutual funds moderated to 11-month low in March, falling sequentially for the third straight month, hurt by lower inflows into sectoral and thematic funds.

Net inflows into equity mutual funds fell 14.4% month-on-month in March to approximately 251 billion rupees ($2.92 billion), the Association of Mutual Funds in India's (AMFI) data showed on Friday.

The moderation in March follows a sharp 26% sequential decline in February, the steepest monthly drop since April 2023, as heightened volatility dampened sentiment.

Flows into mutual funds that focus on particular sectors or themes, which were the top performers in fiscal year 2025, declined 97% year-on-year in March after a 37% fall in February.

AMFI attributed the drop to temporary caution due to market volatility triggered by tariff concerns.

Large-cap funds saw a marginal drop in inflows to 24.79 billion rupees in the reported month, while mid- and small-cap funds grew 0.2% and 10%, garnering 34.15 billion rupees and 40.92 billion rupees, respectively.

The segments had confirmed a technical bear market in February.

"Some investors are capitalizing on deep-value opportunities in the small- and mid-cap segments, while others are opting for the relative stability of large-cap stocks," said Devang Kabra, Partner and Co-Fund Manager at Wallfort PMS.

While inflows moderated, mutual fund investors bought India's equity mutual funds for the 49th straight month.

Analysts expect the positive momentum to return as the recent drop offered attractive opportunities for investors and on India's perceived well-positioning compared to emerging markets peers amid the ongoing global trade adjustments.

The benchmark Nifty 50 index rose 6.3% in March, ending its longest monthly losing streak in nearly 30 years. The small-cap and mid-cap indexes gained 9.5% and 7.8%, respectively.

Contributions via Systematic Investment Plans (SIPs) decreased 0.3% to 259.26 billion rupees in March, while the number of new SIP accounts fell to 81.1 million from February's 82.6 million.

($1 = 86.0380 Indian rupees)

data.title: Pioneer Lithium Says Review of Exploration Data Confirms Extensive Uranium Mineralization at Namibia Project
data.description:
Pioneer Lithium said the review of historical exploration data for the Warmbad uranium project in Namibia confirmed the presence of extensive, thick zones of uranium mineralization, according to a Friday Australian bourse filing.

It secured the data for the project from the Namibian Ministry of Mines and Energy, the filing said.

The firm plans to begin drilling at the project in the second half, and it will seek to validate and extend historical results, enhance geological understanding, and advance the project toward a maiden mineral resource estimate.

It is currently in the process of organizing access to the project area to complete both a radiometric and magnetic airborne survey.

data.title: H&H International's Total Revenue Jumps 10% in Q1; Shares Surge 12%
data.description:
H&H International's total revenue increased 10% to 3.17 billion yuan in the first quarter from 2.87 billion yuan in the year-ago period, according to a Thursday filing with the Hong Kong bourse.

Revenue from its adult nutrition and care products segment inched up 2% to 1.53 billion yuan from 1.50 billion yuan, while its baby nutrition products logged a 25% rise to 1.14 billion yuan from 909.6 million yuan.

Pet nutrition product revenue jumped 8.2% to 500.8 million yuan from 462.9 million yuan a year earlier.

Revenue from mainland China increased 18% to 2.18 billion yuan from 1.85 billion yuan in the prior-year period.

Shares of the baby care company surged 12% during Friday's afternoon trading.

data.title: Nordic Stocks Tipped to Open HigherMarket Talk
data.description: Nordic markets are seen opening higher with IG calling the OMXS30 up 0.9% at around 2301. The relief rally was short-lived and yesterday saw new steep stock market falls in the U.S., SEB analysts say in a note. "The pessimism stems from the fact that recession risks for the United States very much remain." There is still no tariff deal with any country that has received the 90-day tariff suspension while the conflict with China has worsened, SEB says. In Asia, stock markets are mixed. "It's obvious that the market is preparing for worse times. At the same time, not everything is pitch black." Ahead of the stock market opening today, futures are pointing up in both Europe and the U.S. OMXS30 closed at 2280.57, OMXN40 at 2119.65 and OBX at 1329.94. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: Delinhai Environmental Technology Bags Algae Control, Water System Improvement Project
data.description:
Wuxi Delinhai Environmental Technology won the bid for the improvement of Xingyun Lake's In-situ algae control and water system, according to a Shanghai Stock Exchange disclosure on Friday.

The project's budget is approximately 56 million yuan per year, with a service term of two years.

Yuxi City Jiangchuan District Water Resources Bureau is the tenderer of the project, the Chinese pollution control equipment manufacturer said.

The company's shares rose more than 1% in recent trade.

data.title: U.K. Economy Shows Signs of Strength But Tariffs Darken OutlookUpdate
data.description:
By Joshua Kirby

The U.K. economy is growing more strongly than expected but U.S. President Trump's barrage of tariff hikes and pauses threaten to stymie further acceleration ahead.

Gross domestic product increased by 0.5% in February, figures from the Office for National Statistics showed Friday. That was a better performance than the marginal growth economists had expected, and means the U.K. economy is very likely to record growth over the first quarter as a whole, barring an unexpectedly large reversal in March.

February's growth was driven by strong output from Britain's factories, with manufacturing production unexpectedly rising 2.2% on month on the back of a rise in output from auto plants, as well as in electronics and pharmaceuticals, the ONS said.

However, expansion in the British economy risks being dragged down by wider global economic malaise stemming from uncertainty around Washington's trade policy.

After Trump last week unveiled a sweeping package of tariffs on goods imported into the U.S., London's FTSE 100 index suffered four straight days of losses, enduring on Monday its worst trading since the Covid-19 pandemic. Stocks rallied later in the week after Trump agreed to halt a central part of the tariff package amid the turmoil roiling markets at home and across the globe.

But a 10% tariff will remain in place on U.K. goods, as will a separate 25% duty on cars and metals. At more than 15% of the total, the U.S. is the largest export market for the U.K. goods, more than 58 billion pounds ($75.23 billion) worth of which crossed the Atlantic to America in the 12 months to last September, according to figures from the government's business department.

That makes the U.K. less exposed to tariffs than some neighbors, such as Germany, which sent 160 billion euros of goods to the U.S. last year, or Ireland, home to the operations of many American drugmakers. British goods exports also pale next to services exports to the U.S., worth some 124 billion pounds a year, and not liable to Trump's levies. But even a 10% tariff could knock Britain's already fragile economic growth.

And policymakers at the Bank of England could be forced into cutting interest rates more quickly than they had foreseen in a bid to lift some of the smog clouding activity in the country.

"With the risks to the U.K. economy ramped up due to events of the last few weeks, rates could fall harder and more quickly from here," said Michael Field, chief equity strategist at Morningstar.

Write to Joshua Kirby at joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby

data.title: Toymaker Character Group Pulls Guidance Due to Tariff Uncertainty
data.description:
By Anthony O. Goriainoff

Games and toy company Character Group said it was withdrawing guidance for the fiscal year due to market uncertainty stemming from President Trump's tariffs.

The London-listed company said recent events hurt its ability to forecast sales to the U.S. and assess the financial implications. Trump's trade tariffs, and escalating retaliatory measures against them, have hurt global economic stability over a very short period of time, it said.

Character Group said U.S. sales represented around 20% of turnover in fiscal 2024.

Although the effect of tariffs will be felt in the second half, the company said it remained confident that it will be profitable for the year ending Aug. 31 as a whole.

The toymaker said its results for the first half of fiscal 2025 will be in line with both market expectations and the year-prior period, when it reported adjusted pretax profit of 2.1 million pounds ($2.7 million).

Character Group said it would update shareholders as it gathers a clearer picture of the emerging global economic landscape.

"The company continues to have a strong balance sheet with healthy cash balances and the board is confident that it can ride out this storm," it said.

Write to Anthony O. Goriainoff at anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering U.S. politics. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0241 ET - The dollar slides, with the DXY dollar index dropping to its lowest in 21 months against a basket of currencies overnight, after the White House said Thursday that tariffs imposed on China add up to 145%, not the 125% previously indicated. "While this difference is negligible in any practical economic sense, the market reaction showed an increased sensitivity to the risks of a disorderly economic decoupling between the world's two largest economies," Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. This overshadows previous relief about a delay in additional tariffs on countries except China. The DXY dollar index is last down 0.3% at 100.530, having hit a low of 99.705 overnight. The euro rises 0.6% to $1.1261, having hit a three-year high of 1.1373 overnight, according to FactSet. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

0209 ET - Logitech International's decision to withdraw guidance for fiscal 2026 doesn't come as a surprise and seems sensible in light of uncertainty about trade tariffs, Vontobel's Michael Foeth says in a research note. The Swiss maker of computer peripherals generates around a third of its revenue from customers in the U.S., with 40% of its products made in China and the rest in Vietnam, Taiwan, Malaysia, Thailand and Mexico, according to Vontobel. "The high applicable tariffs on products from China and the uncertainty on further tariffs from other countries will inevitably have an impact on gross margins," the analyst says.(adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

1825 ET - Lovesac shares were among those hardest hit in the new tariff world given its exposure to Vietnam, one of the countries most affected by President Trump's tariff plan. Trump on Wednesday afternoon announced a 90-day-pause to some tariffs, allowing Lovesac to bounce back on its Vietnam exposure becoming less of a liability than thought. But it won't be immune to the trade war with China, responsible to around 13% of its products costs, DA Davidson analysts say in a research note. DA Davidson cuts its target price to $24 a share from $35 a share previously. Lovesac closed up 16% to $18.49.(sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1729 ET - Canadian officials need to reach a so-called detente with Beijing, says Alberta Premier Danielle Smith, as western Canada is feeling the pinch from hefty tariffs China has imposed on Canadian canola and pork. She tells reporters in Ottawa that federal officials need to be more vigilant about restricting Chinese exports into the country. "The Americans don't want China dumping goods into Canada as a back door to hollow out [their] manufacturing sector," Smith says. "We have to figure out a pathway for us to be able to have trading relations with both of those nations." She adds China is Alberta's second-largest export market after the U.S. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1727 ET - Canadian premiers want the new federal government in Ottawa, following the April 28 election, to proceed quickly with talks with Trump administration officials on a revised economic deal between the two neighbors, Alberta's leader Danielle Smith tells reporters in Ottawa. President Trump and Canada PM Carney have agreed that such talks take place. Smith says it is paramount that officials bring certainty to the trading relationship, one of the largest in the world. A formal review is scheduled to begin in 2026, but Smith says waiting until then "would be damaging for us, and damaging to the Americans." She adds she's not a fan of Canada's retaliatory tariffs against the U.S. "We should not be cheering on a trade war," Smith says. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1633 ET - The premier of Canada's oil-rich province of Alberta says she's willing to do whatever it takes to allay concerns in eastern Canada about the construction of a crude-carrying pipeline connecting her province to eastern ports. At an event in Ottawa, Danielle Smith says she realizes there's likely objections or concerns about a pipeline that crosses close to Montreal, Canada's second-largest city. Opposition in Quebec played a role in TC Energy's decision last decade to abandon a west-to-east pipeline. "Let's find out the objections and then try to address them," Smith says. "Maybe we took it for granted that projects would be approved. We have learned our lessons." (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1547 ET - Gold futures close up 3.2% to $3,155.20 an ounce, which is a new record for the front-month contract. It's also the biggest move up the front month contract has seen since April 9, 2020. The elation of President Trump pausing the reciprocal tariffs on numerous countries for 90 days gave way to resumed uncertainty on wider markets, with tariffs on China ramping up. "Gold continues to capitalize on its status as the last safe haven standing," says Robert Yawger of Mizuho Securities USA. Yawger adds that gold futures may break through the $3,200 per troy ounce threshold soon. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1435 ET - Apple is widely expected to hike iPhone prices amid higher tariffs on China, but the company has several levers to pull if it wants to avoid lower volumes as a result, Morgan Stanley analysts say in a research note. The company could roughly double its production in India to move away from China and de-risk its U.S. iPhone supply, the analysts estimate. Such a move is fathomable, but could take longer than six to 12 months, they say. In the nearer term, Apple could eliminate some of its lower-storage iPhones to push demand toward its higher-margin, higher storage models. Doing so would raise average selling prices for iPhones, but the company could help offset the synthetically higher prices by leveraging financing plans and higher carrier subsidies and trade-ins.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1402 ET - Tumult in the Treasury market this week revived discussion of whether Treasury securities should be exempt from the calculation of supplementary-leverage ratios that govern how much capital banks must reserve against various types of assets. An exemption can sometimes help smooth market functioning in a crisis, because it frees up banks to hold more low-risk government securities during a selloff, capping a rise in the government's borrowing costs and the economy's closest thing to a risk-free long-term interest rate. Economist Kristin Forbes of MIT is skeptical of a permanent exemption, she tells WSJ, because holding Treasurys does entail risks that banks should take seriously. "I'm not sure we want to set the precedent that Treasurys are risk free," Forbes says. "I'm not sure that sets the right incentives." (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1358 ET - The Canadian dollar strengthens to a near five-month high relative to the U.S. dollar. According to data provided by Tullett Prebon, USDCAD dropped below C$1.40 for the first time since late November, into C$1.39 territory. The WSJ Dollar Index is down 1.34%, and traders resumed a selloff of equities following President Trump's 90-day tariff reprieve. Traders are gravitating toward a Bank of Canada pause next week, following seven straight rate reductions, says Bank of Nova Scotia chief foreign-exchange strategist Shaun Osborne. "But a major driver over the past day or so has been the obvious loss of faith in the USD," Osborne says. (paul.vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)(Paul.Vieira@wsj.com)

1246 ET - Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee says that long-run market-based inflation expectations are a cornerstone of the central bank's process of judging its management of inflation. "If you started to see long-run market based inflation expectations going up … it's an extremely important data point," Goolsbee says at a New York City luncheon. "In a way, that's the Fed's solemn promise," Goolsbee says of predictable long-run inflation expectations. How are these indicators looking a week after the announcement of the 'Liberation Day' tariffs? "Still OK, but a little concerning," Goolsbee says. He notes that surveys and short-term market indicators show some elevated shorter-term inflation expectations, but that market-based measures of longer run inflation remain anchored. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @matt.grossman)

1243 ET - Even with tariffs on China, which were raised by President Trump to over 100% yesterday, the USDA did not adjust its outlook for U.S. soybean export sales for 2024/25 crop in the April WASDE report, which was left showing 1.83 billion bushels. But the USDA seemed to confirm that it had included Chinese tariffs in its assessment, including a note in the report stating that the agency "only considers trade policies that are in effect at the time of publication," and that "unless a formal end date is specified, the report also assumes that these policies remain in place." The USDA did cut its outlook for soybean ending stocks by 5 million bushels, to 375 million bushels. CBOT soybean futures are up 0.8% following the report's release. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: Dollar Slides as U.S.-China Trade Tensions Intensify; Euro JumpsMarket Talk
data.description: The dollar slides, with the DXY dollar index dropping to its lowest in 21 months against a basket of currencies overnight, after the White House said Thursday that tariffs imposed on China add up to 145%, not the 125% previously indicated. "While this difference is negligible in any practical economic sense, the market reaction showed an increased sensitivity to the risks of a disorderly economic decoupling between the world's two largest economies," Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. This overshadows previous relief about a delay in additional tariffs on countries except China. The DXY dollar index is last down 0.3% at 100.530, having hit a low of 99.705 overnight. The euro rises 0.6% to $1.1261, having hit a three-year high of 1.1373 overnight, according to FactSet. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

data.title: Amazon-backed More Retail plans India IPO next year, betting on its hybrid model
data.description:
Amazon-backed More Retail, a key player in India's food and staples market, plans to go public next year and double its store count in five as shoppers keep turning to supermarkets and online buying for daily groceries, a top executive told Reuters.

Mumbai-based More, known for its neighborhood supermarkets and fresh grocery offerings, operates one of the country's largest retail chains, with 775 stores as of its latest count.

The company reported gross sales of nearly 50 billion rupees ($580.11 million) in fiscal 2025, an 11% rise from the previous year.

"Market cycles and volatility are part and parcel of the public markets ... We are targeting an IPO next year subject to market conditions," said Vinod Nambiar, managing director at More, adding that the company's hybrid stores — serving both as physical outlets and fulfillment centers for Amazon Fresh — have higher margins than standalone brick-and-mortar setups.

The listing plans come at a time when investors have been pulling back from Indian equities amid growing fears of a global recession. But Nambiar is betting that the rising popularity of online grocery deliveries will fuel growth.

Same-store sales for More rose 23% in fiscal 2025, and Nambiar expects that momentum to continue.

The company has expanded its partnership with Amazon Fresh , the e-commerce giant's grocery delivery service, and plans to add more than 500 stores across roughly 160 cities to the collaboration over the next 18 months, he said.

More is also leaning into slotted deliveries—a model where customers select a time window for receiving groceries—as its next growth driver. The strategy contrasts with the boom in quick commerce, or 10-minute deliveries, that has reshaped shopping habits in urban India.

The quick delivery model accounted for more than two-thirds of all e-grocery orders last year, with its overall market share growing roughly fivefold to $6 billion–$7 billion since 2022, according to a recent report.

($1 = 86.1900 Indian rupees)
